explanation: The phrase "When A, B and C" implies a global implication: whenever condition A holds, conditions B and C must also hold concurrently. Thus, it translates to "Globally, if prop_0 is true, then prop_1 and prop_2 are also true."

The natural language phrase "When A, B" indicates a global implication: whenever A is true, B must also be true at that same moment. In this case, A is "prop_0 or prop_1" and B is "prop_2 and prop_3". Thus, we translate "A" as (prop_0 | prop_1) and "B" as (prop_2 & prop_3). Combining these with the global implication results in G((prop_0 | prop_1) -> (prop_2 & prop_3)).

The phrase 'When A, B' implies a global implication, meaning 'Globally, if A then B'. In this case, A is 'prop_0 or prop_1' and B is 'prop_2 and prop_3'. Therefore, the LTL formula is G ((prop_0 | prop_1) -> (prop_2 & prop_3)).

The statement "When prop_0 or prop_1, prop_2 and prop_3" means that whenever the condition "prop_0 or prop_1" holds, the condition "prop_2 and prop_3" must also hold at the same time. This is a global implication. The antecedent is "prop_0 or prop_1", translated to (prop_0 | prop_1). The consequent is "prop_2 and prop_3", translated to (prop_2 & prop_3). Combining these with a global implication results in G((prop_0 | prop_1) -> (prop_2 & prop_3)).

The statement "When A, B" is translated as "Globally (A implies B)". In this case, A is "prop_0 or prop_1" and B is "prop_2 and prop_3". So, the LTL formula becomes G((prop_0 | prop_1) -> (prop_2 & prop_3)).

The statement "When A, B" translates to a global implication `G(A -> B)`. In this case, 'A' is "the ventilator is in Start-up or VentilationOff mode" and 'B' is "prop_0 and prop_1". Given that only `prop_0` and `prop_1` are allowed as atomic propositions, we interpret "Start-up mode" as `prop_0` and "VentilationOff mode" as `prop_1` (or vice-versa, as the disjunction remains equivalent). Therefore, 'A' translates to `(prop_0 | prop_1)`. 'B' directly translates to `(prop_0 & prop_1)`. Combining these, the LTL formula becomes `G((prop_0 | prop_1) -> (prop_0 & prop_1))`.

The statement "When A, B" translates to "Globally (A implies B)". In this case, A is "prop_0 or prop_1" and B is "prop_2 and prop_3". Therefore, the LTL formula is G((prop_0 | prop_1) -> (prop_2 & prop_3)).

The phrase 'When A, B' implies that A being true at any point in time leads to B being true at that same point in time. This translates to a global implication 'G (A -> B)'. Here, 'A' is 'prop_0 or prop_1' and 'B' is 'prop_2 and prop_3'.

The natural language phrase 'When A, B' translates to 'Globally, if A then B'. In this case, 'A' is 'prop_0 or prop_1' and 'B' is 'prop_2 and prop_3'. Therefore, the LTL formula is G ((prop_0 | prop_1) -> (prop_2 & prop_3)).

The statement "When A, B" translates to "Globally, if A then B". In this case, A is "prop_0 or prop_1" and B is "prop_2 and prop_3". Thus, the LTL formula is G((prop_0 | prop_1) -> (prop_2 & prop_3)).
